name: python-packaging
description: Create distributable Python packages with proper project structure, setup.py/pyproject.toml, and publishing to PyPI. Use when packaging Python libraries, creating CLI tools, or distributing Python code.
capabilities: Guide creation of Python package structure with src layout or flat layout
Generate pyproject.toml configurations with complete metadata
Create CLI tools using Click or argparse with entry points
Build wheels and source distributions for distribution
Publish packages to PyPI, TestPyPI, or private repositories
Configure automated publishing with GitHub Actions workflows
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill consists entirely of markdown documentation teaching Python packaging best practices. Static analyzer flagged 118 patterns (backticks, URLs, config examples) that are documentation content, not executable code. All findings are false positives from code examples in fenced markdown blocks. No actual security risks detected.
valueStatement: Python developers struggle with complex packaging workflows and inconsistent project structures. This skill provides complete guidance for creating professional Python packages using modern standards like pyproject.toml and setuptools.
userTitle: Build and publish Python packages with PyPI
limitations: Provides documentation and guidance only - does not execute packaging commands
Requires user to have Python and packaging tools installed locally
Does not manage actual PyPI credentials or authentication tokens
Cannot validate package publishing success without user feedback

useCases.title: Library Author Publishing
useCases.description: Create a new Python library for distribution on PyPI with proper project structure, metadata, and automated publishing workflows.

useCases.targetUser: Developers creating developer tools or utilities
useCases.title: CLI Tool Distribution
useCases.description: Package command-line tools with entry points, making them easily installable via pip with executable commands.

useCases.targetUser: Engineering teams managing internal Python packages
useCases.title: Enterprise Package Management
useCases.description: Set up private package indexes and namespace packages for organizing code across multiple teams and repositories.

promptTemplates.title: Create a basic Python package structure
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me create a new Python package called 'my-tool' with a src layout, pyproject.toml using setuptools, and a basic module structure. I want to include pytest for testing.
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginner setting up their first distributable package

promptTemplates.title: Add CLI entry points to existing package
promptTemplates.prompt: I have an existing Python package with utility functions. Show me how to add CLI entry points using Click so users can run 'mytool' from the command line after installation.

promptTemplates.title: Configure automated PyPI publishing
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that builds and publishes my Python package to PyPI when I create a new release. Include package validation and TestPyPI testing steps.

promptTemplates.title: Set up namespace packages for monorepo
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to split my company's codebase into multiple packages (company-core, company-api, company-utils) that share a namespace. Show me the complete project structure and pyproject.toml configurations.

bestPractices: Use src/ layout to prevent accidental imports from uninstalled source directory
Define all metadata in pyproject.toml following PEP 621 for modern packaging
Test package installation in a clean virtual environment before publishing to PyPI
antiPatterns: Do not use setup.py as primary configuration - migrate to pyproject.toml
Avoid flat layout for libraries as it allows importing without installation
Never commit API tokens or credentials - use environment variables for PyPI authentication

faq.question: What is the difference between pyproject.toml and setup.py?
faq.answer: pyproject.toml is the modern standard (PEP 518/621) that declares build requirements and metadata in a single file. setup.py is the legacy approach using executable Python code. New projects should use pyproject.toml with a build backend like setuptools or hatchling.

faq.question: Should I publish to TestPyPI before PyPI?
faq.answer: Yes, always test on TestPyPI first. It is a separate index that mirrors PyPI's functionality. Upload your package there, install it in a clean environment, verify all functionality works, then publish to the real PyPI.

faq.question: How do I include data files in my package?
faq.answer: Use the package-data configuration in pyproject.toml under [tool.setuptools.package-data]. Specify patterns like '*.json' or 'templates/*.html'. Access files at runtime using importlib.resources.files() for modern Python.

faq.question: What version should I start with for my first release?
faq.answer: Start with 0.1.0 for initial releases to indicate the package is in early development. Use semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH). Move to 1.0.0 when you have a stable public API you do not want to break.

faq.question: Can I automatically version my package from git tags?
faq.answer: Yes, use setuptools-scm in your build-system.requires. Configure it in pyproject.toml to read version information from git tags. This ensures version matches git history and automates version management.

faq.question: How do I create a CLI tool that users can install with pip?
faq.answer: Define entry points in pyproject.toml under [project.scripts]. Map a command name to a Python function path like 'mytool = my_package.cli:main'. After installation, users can run 'mytool' from their terminal.
